title: The Stakeout

jokes.text: Hos before bros. Uteruses before duderuses. Ovaries before brovaries.
jokes.setup: Leslie expressing her 'code' about friendship priorities
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd rhyming variations on 'bros before hos'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay with perfect escalation structure. Each variation gets more ridiculous while maintaining the rhyme scheme. High quotability factor.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so my code is: bros before hos.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Flows before foes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Glows before grows.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Crows before crows.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's just crows twice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know, I'm running out of words.

jokes.text: The thing is, Mark isn't my ex. We slept together six years ago. Anyway, I'm over it. Or am I? I'm just kidding.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining she's totally fine about Mark dating Ann
jokes.punchline: Her obvious overthinking undermines her claim of being 'over it'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's tendency to overthink and contradict herself when nervous.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm totally fine about Mark dating Ann. It doesn't bother me at all. I mean, why would it? They're both adults. Mark's a great guy, Ann's a great girl. They're great together. I'm genuinely happy for them.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But if you think about it, Mark's only been single for like two weeks, and Ann just got out of her thing with Tom, so statistically speaking, relationships that start this fast have a 73% failure rate. And Mark's not even that great. I mean, he's fine. He's fine.

jokes.text: Those are, of course, tomatoes. Or souljaboy tellems.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking Tom for the Latin names of plants
jokes.punchline: Tom making up fake Latin names using rapper names
jokes.explanation: Creative premise with excellent character integration. Tom's vanity and pop culture obsession perfectly expressed.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, what are the Latin names of these plants?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, that one's Biggie Smalls Botanicus, and that one right there is Tupac Shakur Leaficus.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, those aren't real Latin names.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Are you sure? Because I'm ninety percent confident in my botanical knowledge.

jokes.text: Whenever Leslie asks me for the Latin names of our plants, I just give her the names of rappers.
jokes.setup: Tom's talking head explaining his plant naming strategy
jokes.punchline: Revealing the systematic deception behind his fake expertise
jokes.explanation: Solid character reveal that enhances the previous joke by showing Tom's deliberate mischief.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I name all my plants after famous people. That way, when they die, it just sounds like they got famous and moved to New York or something.

jokes.text: Those are some diddies. There's some bonethugs and harmoniums right here. Those ludacrises are coming in great.
jokes.setup: Continuation of the fake Latin plant names bit
jokes.punchline: More elaborate rapper name puns as plant species
jokes.explanation: Good extension of the established bit. 'Harmoniums' is particularly clever wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I got more. Notorious B.I.G. Leaf. Tupac Shakur-cula. Snoop Dogg Rose. 50 Cent Plant. Jay-Z-anthemum. Eminem-osa. Drake-aena. Post Malone-y Plant. Lil Nas X-icum. And my personal favorite, Cardi B-gone, the plant that disappears after two weeks.

jokes.text: You know, the best way to figure out what kind of spice that is, is roll it up into a joint and smoke it.
jokes.setup: The group discussing what the mysterious plant might be
jokes.punchline: Tom's casually inappropriate suggestion about drug use
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate but relatively predictable stoner humor suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's probably just some kind of exotic plant. Or maybe it's a drug plant. You know, like marijuana or cocaine or something. I'm not saying we should use it, I'm just saying if we did, I bet it would be really good.

jokes.text: Did you get my text? Did you get my emails? Did you see that I paged you? I did not. Did you check your voicemail? I didn't.
jokes.setup: Leslie frantically listing all her communication attempts
jokes.punchline: Ron's complete indifference to all forms of contact
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast. Ron's deadpan delivery against Leslie's manic energy creates great comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've called you 47 times. I've left 23 voicemails. I've sent 19 emails, 12 texts, and I even tried sending a fax to your office, but apparently you don't have a fax machine anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I've been ignoring all of it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But Ron, this is important!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Leslie, I don't check my phone, I don't read emails, and I certainly don't read faxes. I'm a simple man. I like breakfast foods and woodworking. That's it.

jokes.text: Then there'll be an investigation. And they'll find my fingerprints on the manure.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining why they can't call the police
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous detail about fingerprints on manure
jokes.explanation: Absurd but logical-sounding paranoia that's perfectly in character for Leslie's overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We can't call the police. There are fingerprints on the manure.

jokes.text: Ron slowly explaining his hernia situation while sitting perfectly still
jokes.setup: Ron's mysterious absence and strange behavior
jokes.punchline: The visual of Ron frozen in place due to a hernia
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy enhanced by Ron's characteristic stoicism in the face of physical discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm fine. Just a little stiff.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, you can barely move. We need to get you to a doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Absolutely not. Swanson men don't go to doctors. We tough it out.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Dude, you're literally frozen. You haven't blinked in twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's called focus.

jokes.text: It's a 13-year-old kid named Stevie who likes to get high and make his transformers look like they're having sex.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about the criminal 'kingpin'
jokes.punchline: The anticlimactic and weirdly specific description of a kid
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of expectations with hilariously specific and inappropriate detail.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So what's this kingpin like? Is he dangerous?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, extremely dangerous. He's a 14-year-old who sells bootleg Pokemon cards out of his backpack.

jokes.text: I would like to be president some day, so, no, I have not smoked marijuana. I ate a brownie once. At a party in college. It was intense. It was kind of indescribable, actually. I felt like I was floating. Turns out there wasn't any pot in it. It was just an insanely good brownie.
jokes.setup: Leslie's serious denial about drug use
jokes.punchline: The elaborate story that ends with it being just a regular brownie
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with excellent character integration. Leslie's earnestness makes the punchline land harder.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have never used drugs. Not even once. Well, except for that time in college when I thought I was at a rave, but it turned out to be a Christian youth group meeting. So I ate what I thought was molly, but it was just a brownie. A regular brownie.

jokes.text: Get my lunch for me, please. Okay, like order you something? No, get it... from there.
jokes.setup: Ron asking April to get his lunch
jokes.punchline: Ron pointing to his desk because he can't move due to hernia
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback that shows Ron's stubborn refusal to admit vulnerability.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: April, could you get my lunch from my desk?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why can't you get it yourself?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a hernia.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Of course you do.

jokes.text: I have stakeout supplies. Notepads, pencils, and pencil case. Shakable whipped cream. Cameras. Gorp. And... candy necklaces.
jokes.setup: Leslie listing professional stakeout equipment
jokes.punchline: The increasingly ridiculous and non-police items
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's amateur enthusiasm and complete misunderstanding of police work.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've got binoculars, a camera with a telephoto lens, night vision goggles, a two-way radio, a thermal imaging device, a GPS tracker, a voice recorder, seventeen energy bars, a laminated map of the city, a disguise kit, a grappling hook, a flare gun, and a jetpack.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, why do you have a jetpack?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I don't ask questions about your equipment choices.

jokes.text: And I made us a mix CD. It's all filled with songs about people watching people. It's mostly Sting.
jokes.setup: Leslie's continued stakeout preparation
jokes.punchline: The overly literal and nerdy approach to stakeout music
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Leslie's overthinking and literal approach to everything. 'It's mostly Sting' is a great specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, for a stakeout you need the right music. Something that gets your adrenaline pumping but keeps you alert.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've created the perfect playlist: 'Mission: Impossible' theme on repeat for six hours, followed by the entire 'Rocky' soundtrack.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, that's going to drive us insane.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's the point! You can't fall asleep if you're slowly going insane. It's science.

jokes.text: Tommy Hilfiger? No, Tom Haverford. I spent 120 bucks to get it monogrammed. Everyone thinks it's Hilfiger.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking about Tom's shirt
jokes.punchline: Tom's expensive vanity project that accidentally mimics a famous brand
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy that perfectly captures Tom's vanity and lack of self-awareness. The accidental brand confusion is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, that's a really nice shirt. Where'd you get it?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: This? I had it custom made. Cost me $800.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: $800? For a shirt?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: It's a Tom's Luxury Goods original. I designed it myself. Sustainable fabrics, hand-stitched details...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, that's the Gucci logo.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, it's not. It's a double G. For 'Gorgeous Guy.'

jokes.text: When Andy and I used to go to the movies, he would always try to guess the ending of the movie. And he would always guess that the main character had been dead the whole time. Even when we saw Ratatouille.
jokes.setup: Ann explaining Andy's movie habits
jokes.punchline: Andy thinking the rat in Ratatouille was dead the whole time
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke with perfect structure and timing. The Ratatouille detail is absurd and specific enough to be memorable and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Andy watched Ratatouille and thought the rat was dead the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, he was very still!

jokes.text: You know, with a body like this, you don't get the brains too. That was silly.
jokes.setup: Ann making what seems like a self-deprecating comment
jokes.punchline: Her immediate recognition that it made no sense
jokes.explanation: Mild self-aware humor that shows Ann catching herself being nonsensical.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm like a human mosquito.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Wait, that doesn't make any sense.

jokes.text: Brendanawicz is the man. This is Brendanawicz's life: hot chick from the newspaper, hot chick from the post office, hot chick from the hospital. Her name's Ann. You know her name. Her name's Ann.
jokes.setup: Tom describing Mark's dating success
jokes.punchline: Tom's increasing frustration as he realizes he's reducing Ann to 'hot chick'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tom getting called out on his casual objectification.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Mark's out there dating all these beautiful women, and I'm like, 'How is this happening?' And then I realized - Mark doesn't see them as people. He just sees them as... hot chicks. And I was like, 'That's the secret!' So I tried it. I went up to a woman and I was like, 'Hey, you're a hot chick.' And she was like, 'That's not a compliment.' And I was like, 'No, no, no, you don't understand. I'm reducing you to your physical appearance so I can date you.' Still didn't work.

jokes.text: We were drunk, and he tried to kiss me, and I said, 'nuh-uh, no, sir, Mark Brendanawicz. I don't need your business here.'
jokes.setup: Leslie claiming she turned down Mark
jokes.punchline: The overly formal and awkward phrasing of her rejection
jokes.explanation: Excellent character voice. The formal business-like rejection language is perfectly Leslie and highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I said, 'Mark, I appreciate your interest in me as a romantic partner, but I must respectfully decline your proposal of courtship at this time, as I am currently focused on my career in municipal government and do not wish to compromise my professional standing or emotional availability.'

jokes.text: Get down!
jokes.setup: Leslie and Tom on their amateur stakeout
jokes.punchline: Leslie's over-dramatic spy movie reaction to seeing people
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy showing Leslie's amateur detective enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God. Tom, do you see what I see?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, I see it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is it. This is actually happening. We're doing it. We're really doing it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Leslie, it's just people walking.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Just people walking? JUST people walking?! Tom, we are living in a John le Carré novel right now!

jokes.text: Shovel guitar. Somebody wants to play shovel guitar. Leslie's been playing shovel guitar for about an hour now. Bucket drum.
jokes.setup: Andy living in the pit and making music
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous image of makeshift instruments from construction tools
jokes.explanation: Great visual comedy and character moment. Andy's resourcefulness in making music from anything fits perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I've been working on some new songs down here in the pit. I made a drum set out of old pipes and a bucket, and I'm using a shovel as a guitar.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I call my band 'The Pit Dwellers.' We're gonna be huge.

jokes.text: You're not from here, right? No, I'm from South Carolina. But you moved to South Carolina from where? My mother's uterus. But you were conceived in Libya?
jokes.setup: Andy's questions about Tom's background getting increasingly specific
jokes.punchline: Tom's literal answer about being from his mother's uterus
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation with Tom's deadpan literal response cutting through Andy's weird interrogation.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Where are you from, Tom?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: South Carolina.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, like, what part of South Carolina?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Myrtle Beach area.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, but where in Myrtle Beach? Like what neighborhood?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: My mother's uterus.

jokes.text: My birth name is... Darwish Zubair Ismail Gani. Then I changed it to Tom Haverford because, you know, brown guys with funny-sounding Muslim names don't make it really far in politics.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining his name change
jokes.punchline: The blunt acknowledgment of racial prejudice in politics
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary delivered with Tom's characteristic bluntness. Good character development disguised as humor.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I changed my name from Tom Hapablap to Tom Haverford because I wanted to go into politics. And in politics, you can't have a name like Hapablap. You just can't. Sorry, but that's the truth.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hapablap doesn't poll well in focus groups.

jokes.text: What about Barack Obama? Yeah, fine, Barack Obama. If I knew a dude named Barack Obama was gonna be elected president, maybe I wouldn't have changed it.
jokes.setup: Andy pointing out the obvious counterexample to Tom's theory
jokes.punchline: Tom's grudging acknowledgment with hindsight regret
jokes.explanation: Decent topical humor that shows Tom's tendency toward dramatic overreaction and regret.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But what about Ben? He's a nerd and he's cool.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah... I should've thought of that before I spent all that money on this leather jacket.

jokes.text: White male, light brown hair. Just take pictures. Oh, my God. It looks like Andy. That is Andy.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the 'criminal' they're surveilling
jokes.punchline: The reveal that their target is actually Andy
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection and reveal that advances the plot while providing a solid comedic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We've been tracking this guy for weeks. Multiple counts of reckless behavior, complete disregard for protocol, and an almost supernatural ability to cause chaos wherever he goes.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Sounds dangerous. Who is it?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy.

jokes.text: Andy is the kingpin.
jokes.setup: The dramatic investigation into drug dealing
jokes.punchline: Describing hapless Andy as a 'kingpin'
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and absurdist contrast between the serious terminology and Andy's harmless nature.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, you're being investigated as a drug kingpin.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What? A kingpin? That sounds cool!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's not cool, Andy. It's a federal crime.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But 'kingpin' has 'king' in it...

jokes.text: You're living in the pit now? For now, yeah. It's awesome. Somebody planted a garden down there with fruits and vegetables, so I'm getting a lot of vitamins.
jokes.setup: The discovery that Andy is homeless and living in a pit
jokes.punchline: Andy's relentlessly positive spin on his dire situation
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Andy's ability to find silver linings in terrible situations.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm not homeless, I'm just... houseless. Which is actually pretty great because I don't have to pay rent, and I can see the stars every night.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, you're living in a pit.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A pit with a view!

jokes.text: Don't look at me. There's weed down there? I thought that was the tops of carrots.
jokes.setup: Andy being accused of planting marijuana
jokes.punchline: His genuine confusion about what marijuana looks like
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Andy's innocence and obliviousness to drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what does marijuana even look like? Like, is it just a regular plant? Because I thought it was like a... a purple flower or something.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, no.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Or a cactus? It could be a cactus.

jokes.text: Do you live here? Yeah, do you live here? Catch.
jokes.setup: April's direct question to homeless Andy
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact rock throwing that follows
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction showing April's blunt approach and the casual weirdness of the rock fight.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm fine.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're living in a pit.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's not a pit, it's a shallow hole.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're an idiot.

jokes.text: I went home, but I had this strange feeling that there was something wrong with you, so I came back.
jokes.setup: April explaining why she returned
jokes.punchline: Her blunt assessment that something was 'wrong' with Ron
jokes.explanation: Perfect April delivery showing her unusual form of caring through blunt assessment.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I came back because I realized something was wrong with Ron.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What do you mean something's wrong with me?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're being nice to people. You smiled at a child. You complimented Leslie's hair. That's not you.

jokes.text: AIDS? No, I'm safe. Blindness? Is it like a parasite or a virus or something you get from a bee? I have a hernia. Do you have syphilis? I said it's a hernia. I know. It's possible to have two things.
jokes.setup: April trying to diagnose Ron's medical issue
jokes.punchline: Her increasingly absurd suggestions and logic about multiple conditions
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with perfect character voices. April's deadpan logic about having multiple diseases is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: So based on your symptoms, you either have scurvy, or you've been cursed by a witch.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have a cold.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's what a witch wants you to think. Also, you might be a werewolf. Have you checked?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm going to the doctor.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Doctors are witches. It's a whole thing.

jokes.text: But I rode my bike, so I have to go home and get my dad's station wagon.
jokes.setup: April offering Ron a ride to the hospital
jokes.punchline: The impractical transportation logistics she needs to work out first
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing April's well-meaning but impractical help.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I can give you a ride to the hospital, but I'm gonna need to stop at home first to pick up my emotional support snake, then I have to drop off my tax returns at my accountant's office on the other side of town, and then I need to pick up some dark lipstick because I'm out, so it's gonna be like an hour and a half.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'll call an ambulance.

jokes.text: I miss her so much, it's ridiculous. How's she doing? Doing good tonight. She's out on a... Ann is great. And I bet she really misses you.
jokes.setup: Andy asking about Ann while Tom knows she's on a date with Mark
jokes.punchline: Tom's awkward attempt to avoid revealing the truth
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Tom trying to be kind while hiding painful information.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Tom, have you seen Ann around? I wanted to grab lunch with her.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann? No, I... I haven't seen Ann. She's probably... doing Ann things.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Ann things? What does that even mean?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know, the things that Ann does. Alone. By herself. Definitely not with anyone else, especially not with Mark.

jokes.text: You know there's a string in there. Not in this one. Instant sugar high. Sugar high. Sugar hit high. Sugar high. High. Sugar slam.
jokes.setup: Andy eating a candy necklace
jokes.punchline: His increasingly manic reaction to sugar and the string realization
jokes.explanation: Great physical comedy showing Andy's childlike enthusiasm and immediate sugar rush.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: This is the best thing I've ever eaten! Oh man, oh man, oh man!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait... I'm eating string? I'm eating string?!

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No no no, that's fine, that's fine, I'm gonna keep going, this is amazing!

jokes.text: I have been a perfect gentleman this evening. In fact, watch this. I would like you to now please invite me in for a nightcap. Not a chance. No. Just watch... watch what I'm gonna do. Ask me in for a nightcap. Will you come in for a nightcap? Yes, I will. No, no, damn. Drats, I was gonna plan to not.
jokes.setup: Mark trying to demonstrate his gentlemanly restraint
jokes.punchline: His plan immediately falling apart when presented with the opportunity
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure showing Mark's good intentions undermined by his instant cave to temptation.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I'm going to show you how a gentleman behaves. I will not make a move on you, no matter what.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Okay...

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: I have incredible self-control and discipline.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's great, Mark.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: So if you were to, say, kiss me right now, I would politely decline and—

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm not going to do that.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: No, no, no. I'm just saying hypothetically. If you wanted to—

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I don't.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: But if you did, I would—actually, you know what? Forget the hypothetical. Just kiss me.

jokes.text: So, do you like spy on Ann? From the pit? I just like being nearby. That way if she wants me back, I could be at her house in two seconds, before she changes her mind.
jokes.setup: Tom asking about Andy's living situation
jokes.punchline: Andy's creepy but innocent stalker-adjacent logic
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy that's both disturbing and endearing due to Andy's innocent delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So where are you living these days?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm staying at April's place.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: April's? How'd you swing that?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, I just showed up at her door with my stuff, and she didn't say no. So technically, she said yes.

jokes.text: She's dating somebody else? I didn't say that. Who is she dating? What? Is she dating somebody else? I didn't say that. Who is she dating? Nobody. Mark.
jokes.setup: Tom trying to avoid revealing Ann's date
jokes.punchline: Andy's persistence forcing Tom to accidentally reveal the truth
jokes.explanation: Good cringe escalation showing Tom's inability to keep secrets under pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Come on, Tom, just tell me who Ann's going out with!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Andy, I can't do that. Ann told me in confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Please? I really need to know!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No way. My lips are sealed.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Is it someone I know? Just give me a hint!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Andy, stop asking me questions or I'm going to accidentally—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Accidentally what?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Tell you it's Jerry.

jokes.text: I need you to step out and show me some ID. It's okay, my name's Tom Haverford. I work for the Parks Department. I got locked out of the van. I had to jimmy my way back in. Why don't you jimmy your way out and show me some ID? I just told you my ID, so what's the crime here? Parking while Indian?
jokes.setup: Tom being questioned by police for suspicious activity
jokes.punchline: Tom's sharp comeback about racial profiling
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary with excellent wordplay. Tom's quick wit in a stressful situation shows great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: We've had reports of suspicious activity in this area. Can you explain what you're doing here?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Suspicious activity? I'm standing on a public street in my own neighborhood. Unless you're profiling me because of how I look, in which case, I've got a whole list of complaints I can file right now.

jokes.text: There's no stereotypes about Indians sitting in vehicles.
jokes.setup: Tom's accusation of racial profiling
jokes.punchline: The officer's weak and technically literal defense
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response that highlights the absurdity of the officer's position.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Dunn
dialogue.line: Are you seriously profiling me right now? This is racial profiling!

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: No, no. I'm profiling you because you're suspicious, not because you're black.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Dunn
dialogue.line: How is that better?!

jokes.text: Nice job, Paul Blart. Why don't you head back to the mall, make sure nobody's breaking into lady-foot locker?
jokes.setup: Tom's escalating conflict with the police officer
jokes.punchline: The specific Paul Blart reference with the creative mall cop insult
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture reference that fits Tom's character, though somewhat dated and predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You know what? I've had it with you. You're like Paul Blart, except instead of riding a Segway through a mall, you ride your own ego through this town.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Did you just compare me to Paul Blart?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you know what the worst part is? Paul Blart would actually be more effective at this job.

jokes.text: I'll step out of your mama's van.
jokes.setup: Officer demanding Tom step out of the van
jokes.punchline: Tom's juvenile 'your mama' comeback in a serious situation
jokes.explanation: Weak joke that works mainly due to the inappropriate timing and Tom's character.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: Step out of the van.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Your mama step out of the van.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm just... your mama jokes are timeless, officer.

jokes.text: Your name's Dave? Can I call you Dave? My mother likes David, but I'm pretty split on... I hate to break it to you. You really stepped in it. What? I'm a government employee, and so is your prisoner. If I wanted to, I could get on the horn, and I could have Ron Swanson down here. Kicking down your doors. That's right, you heard me.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to intimidate the police officer
jokes.punchline: Her threatening to call Ron Swanson as if he's a powerful enforcer
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Leslie's inflated sense of Parks Department power. The Ron Swanson threat is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm going to call Ron Swanson.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: Who's Ron Swanson?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Parks and Recreation. Do I stutter?
jokes.setup: Officer asking what branch of government Leslie works for
jokes.punchline: Leslie's tough-guy delivery about Parks and Recreation
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character integration. Leslie treating Parks and Rec like it's the FBI is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: What branch of government do you work for?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Parks and Recreation.

dialogue.speaker: Officer
dialogue.line: No, I mean like, federal, state, local...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh. Local. Parks and Recreation Department. It's the best job in the world.

jokes.text: We also found an enormous amount of manure, and a very inappropriate amount of candy.
jokes.setup: Officer listing suspicious items found in the van
jokes.punchline: The formal description of candy as 'inappropriate' in quantity
jokes.explanation: Excellent deadpan delivery making normal items sound sinister. 'Very inappropriate amount of candy' is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: We found what appears to be approximately 200 pounds of candy in the van.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna have to cite you for transporting an inappropriate quantity of candy.

jokes.text: I took the pictures. My best friend was going on a date with my ex-lover.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining the suspicious surveillance photos
jokes.punchline: Her overly dramatic description of Mark as her 'ex-lover'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's tendency to overdramatize casual encounters.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: These photos show Mark leaving my apartment at 6 AM. Now, I know what you're thinking, but Mark is not my ex-lover. He's my ex-employee. Well, actually, he's my current employee. Well, he's my... he's my something. The point is, these photos are very misleading.

jokes.text: Prison changes a man. I think he'll wanna see a familiar face when he gets back on the outside.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining why she's waiting for Tom
jokes.punchline: Treating a brief police detention like a long prison sentence
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy showing Leslie's dramatic interpretation of minor events.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom's running a little late. He got detained by police.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Don't worry, it's not serious. He'll be out in like twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Although, knowing Tom, he's probably in there telling everyone about his business plan and asking if they have any connections in the music industry.

jokes.text: I'm Ron [beep] Swanson.
jokes.setup: Ron being helped to April's car while injured
jokes.punchline: His tough-guy declaration while obviously helpless
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. Ron's stubborn machismo even when clearly vulnerable and injured.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm fine. I don't need help. I can walk to that car myself.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Ron, you can barely stand.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm a man. I can push through the pain. Now if you'll excuse me, I need to sit down for a moment.

jokes.text: You kind of kissed me. Yes, I did, and now we both have herpes.
jokes.setup: Mark and Ann acknowledging their kiss
jokes.punchline: Mark's immediate jump to the worst possible consequence
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor callback to the 'terrible things happened last time' setup.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: So... that happened.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: Yeah. We should probably talk about this.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I mean, we work together, so—

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: We're gonna get fired, aren't we? We're both getting fired. I'm gonna lose my job, my house, and I'll probably end up living in a box under the Ramsett Park overpass.

jokes.text: These are... carrots. You don't think I know the difference between carrots and marijuana?
jokes.setup: The investigation concluding with the plants being examined
jokes.punchline: Leslie's indignant defense of her botanical knowledge when she was wrong
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's inability to admit mistakes even when caught.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know plants! I have a very extensive knowledge of botanical species and their care requirements. I've read numerous gardening books and I've been maintaining a thriving indoor garden for years!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Those plants died because of external factors completely beyond my control, not because of anything I did wrong!

jokes.text: You wanted to check up on your friend and that guy, your ex, so you made up a story about weed so you could stake out their date. I did not. There actually was weed here. It's okay. I think it's kind of cute.
jokes.setup: Dave figuring out Leslie's real motivation
jokes.punchline: Leslie's weak denial followed by Dave finding it endearing
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the episode's central conceit with romantic subplot development.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: You only care about this because you want to be the hero of the story.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That is... not true. I care about the park. And the community. And...

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: And being the hero.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: ...okay, yes. But is that so bad?

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: No. Actually, it's kind of great.

jokes.text: I like Miss Knope. I gotta say when I first met her, I didn't care much for her, because, like 99% of the people in any given day of my life, she was very belligerent and disagreeable. Miss Knope was attractive to me as a man. I was attracted to her in her demeanor. I was attracted to her in a sexual manner that was appropriate. I don't want to talk about this anymore.
jokes.setup: Dave giving his assessment of Leslie
jokes.punchline: His increasingly awkward attempts to explain his attraction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing a tough cop getting flustered trying to explain romantic feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Leslie is... she's got this energy, you know? She's very... enthusiastic.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: And she's smart. Very smart. And determined. She really commits to things.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: She's got great... she's very... I mean, physically, she's...

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Look, I'm just saying I find her very attractive and it's making this really hard.

jokes.text: What a crazy night. Partner? Want to go get some breakfast? What? No. Take me home. What is wrong with you? Oh, my God, I can't believe this is on! I wonder if mini golf is open. Home!
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to bond with Tom after their adventure
jokes.punchline: Tom's increasingly frantic attempts to get home and Leslie's oblivious chatter
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast showing Leslie's energy vs Tom's exhaustion after trauma.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That was such an amazing day! We really bonded out there. I feel like we're basically best friends now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's great. Hey, I should probably get going though.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Already? But I was thinking we could grab dinner and talk about our feelings and dreams!

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I really can't. I have to... I have plans.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, what kind of plans? Because I'm free all week. We could do a whole trip! Remember how fun that was?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: No, no, no—very important plans. Work stuff. I gotta go right now actually.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You seem anxious. Is this about your feelings? We can talk through this.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm not anxious, I'm just—LESLIE, I have to leave. Like, immediately.

jokes.text: We didn't think you were a pervert. We thought you were a criminal. For the record, I still actually kind of think he's a pervert.
jokes.setup: Tom confronting them about the arrest
jokes.punchline: Mark's blunt assessment after Ann's polite correction
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Mark's characteristically blunt honesty undermining Ann's diplomacy.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: We weren't arrested, we were just detained for questioning.

dialogue.speaker: Mark
dialogue.line: You were arrested.

tmdb_overview: While tending to the new community garden, Leslie and Tom discover someone has planted marijuana among the vegetables. In an effort to catch the people responsible, they pull an all-night stakeout. With Leslie's permission, Ann decides to go on a date with Mark.
insight: Leslie's enthusiasm drives 80-plus comedy through pure character-based absurdism and deadpan restraint.
